content.problemStatement:
As usual, Tom is trying to catch Jerry. To accomplish this task, Tom hired KKK other cats (Tom does not take part in the chase). Formally, they are in a graph GGG with NNN vertices and MMM edges. Let's denote the set of vertices of GGG by VVV. The graph has a special property: for each simple cycle containing at least four vertices, there is an edge which is not part of this cycle, but connects two distinct vertices on the cycle.

Jerry can only move along the edges of the graph, but arbitrarily fast. The cats don't have to follow the edges — they can use a helicopter to go to any vertices they want.

The process of catching Jerry happens in several rounds as follows:


In round 000, the cats choose a set of vertices X0⊆VX_0 \subseteq VX0​⊆V with ∣X0∣≤K|X_0| \le K∣X0​∣≤K; Jerry chooses a vertex v0∈V∖X0v_0 \in V \setminus X_0v0​∈V∖X0​.
For each i≥0i \ge 0i≥0, let's denote the set of vertices containing cats in round iii by XiX_iXi​ and the vertex containing Jerry by viv_ivi​. In round i+1i+1i+1, the cats choose a subset Xi+1⊆VX_{i+1} \subseteq VXi+1​⊆V with ∣Xi+1∣≤K|X_{i+1}| \le K∣Xi+1​∣≤K.
Afterwards in round i+1i+1i+1, Jerry must choose a vertex vi+1∈V∖Xi+1v_{i+1} \in V \setminus X_{i+1}vi+1​∈V∖Xi+1​ such that there is a path from viv_ivi​ to vi+1v_{i+1}vi+1​ which doesn't pass through any vertex in Xi∩Xi+1X_i \cap X_{i+1}Xi​∩Xi+1​. If Jerry cannot choose such a vertex, the cats win.
If this process can last forever (KKK cats are not enough to catch Jerry), then Jerry wins.

Your task is to calculate the minimum number of cats KKK which Tom has to call in order to guarantee that Jerry will be caught (there is no winning strategy for Jerry).
constraints: there is at most one edge between each pair of vertices
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers N and M. M lines follow. Each of these lines contains two space-separated integers u and v denoting an edge between vertices u and v.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the minimum number of cats needed to catch Jerry.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The minimum number of cats needed to catch Jerry is 3, because we can place one cat at each of the vertices 1, 2, and 3, and Jerry will be caught.
The minimum number of cats needed to catch Jerry is 3, because we can place one cat at each of the vertices 1, 3, and 5, and Jerry will be caught.
The minimum number of cats needed to catch Jerry is 3, because we can place one cat at each of the vertices 1, 3, and 5, and Jerry will be caught.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the concept of minimum vertex cover in graph theory. The minimum vertex cover of a graph is the smallest set of vertices such that every edge in the graph is incident to at least one vertex in the set. In this problem, we need to find the minimum number of cats (vertices) needed to catch Jerry, which is equivalent to finding the minimum vertex cover of the graph.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*(N+M)) — where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of vertices, and M is the number of edges. The time complexity is dominated by the graph traversal and the minimum vertex cover algorithm.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N+M) — where N is the number of vertices and M is the number of edges. The space complexity is dominated by the graph representation and the minimum vertex cover algorithm.
